reasoning:
The Chicago Fed's National Financial Conditions Risk Subindex (NFCIRISK) tracks financial sector risk and volatility. Historically, it is a relatively smooth time series with strong autocorrelation and momentum. Currently, it is in a rapid and consistent downtrend, decreasing from -0.576 in October 2025 to -0.619 by mid-December 2025, dropping by nearly 0.01 per week in the most recent weeks. 

For the 1-week resolution date (Dec 28), it is highly likely that this strong downward momentum will continue as there are no obvious scheduled macroeconomic shocks and year-end effects don't show a strong historical tendency to cause an immediate spike in this specific index; thus, the probability of an increase is very low (15%).

By the 4-week mark (Jan 20), uncertainty increases somewhat, allowing for typical new-year repositioning or unexpected economic data, but momentum often persists over a month (28%). 

By the 13-week mark (Mar 21), a reversion to the mean or a shift in the macroeconomic regime is much more plausible. Since the index is currently quite low at -0.619 (indicating very loose conditions compared to the historical average of 0), a tightening of risk conditions over a 3-month horizon is almost a coin toss, pushing the probability up to 45%.
